summary: This is a science fiction short story, not a typical Yegge blog post. Set roughly a thousand years in the future, it follows the 'death' of Richard Dawkins—reimagined as a near-immortal saint of the 'Church of Modern Atheism'—during an extreme endurance race. The story weaves together multiple threads: two desperate Posey (poor human) boys stealing mechs to destroy Dawkins' data center, a tourist family experiencing the event as a historical tour, and Dawkins himself trapped in his biological race body. The narrative explores a post-Singularity world where most humans have migrated online, consciousness can be backed up, and the boundaries between life and death have become administrative matters.
key_points: The story imagines a future where atheism became an organized religion (COMA) with Dawkins as a canonized saint, which he opposed
Humanity has largely migrated online ('Rosies'), leaving only poor 'Poseys' in physical form, while 'Rivers' are conscious superorganisms evolved from social networks
The Singularity exists but is resource-limited and uses human interactions to generate truly random numbers via 'External Events'
Death has become complicated—Dawkins dies physically but exists as an 'External' entity that orchestrates mysterious phenomena
The ending suggests redemption as the boys who caused Dawkins' death are mysteriously granted enough money to become Rosies
money_quotes: Most of his long life, he reflected lazily, had been focused on other people. Educating them, mostly. It was a shame how often he'd had to resort to the switch, the rod, the metaphorical paddle. People just didn't want to learn.
Science advanced, technology advanced, but every generation was every bit as wilfully ignorant as its predecessors.
COMA hadn't killed theism. It had merely added itself to the pantheon for nine-tenths of the world's publicly comatheistic but privately polytheistic inhabitants.
Like all important races, it only ended when you died.
Being alone in his body felt absurdly like an out-of-body experience.
Between the Rivers and the Singularity, the demand for computing resources—with its consequent energy consumption and heat dissipation—had nearly killed the planet.
key_insight: Even in a future where death is largely optional and atheism has become an organized religion, human nature—with its ignorance, desperation, and capacity for unexpected grace—remains fundamentally unchanged.
